msContents.summary: Sefer Mitsṿot Ḳaṭan (SeMaK) by Isaac ben Joseph of Corbeil, and Sefer Tashbets by Samson ben Zadok

msItem.title: The Small Book of Commandments
ספר מצוות קטן
Sefer Mitsṿot Ḳaṭan

author.persName: Isaac ben Joseph, of Corbeil, active 13th century

msItem.note: With the annotations of Perez ben Elijah, of Corbeil and a few other glosses.

msItem.title: תשב"ץ
Tashbets
msItem.note: With glosses by Perez ben Elijah, of Corbeil.

handDesc.handNote: The colophon at the end of no. 1, which is written in micrography on f. 155r, indicates only the scribe's name, Yehudah, which is frequently pointed out in the copied text (e.g. fols. 29r, 103v, 162v). Many of the enlarged initial words have not been executed as planned.

decoDesc.decoNote: Simple, abstract penwork decoration above chapter numbers and around catchwords, often formed from short, tick-like strokes; embellished descenders for letters at the bottom of the text block; near the end of the SeMaK, carmen figuratum in the form of a pitcher on f. 154r, and two full-page abstract micrographical designs on ff. 154v, 155r.

bindingDesc.p: Dark brown blind-tooled leather over wooden boards, rebacked with an alum-tawed skin in a quarter leather style.

origin.note: The first letter of divorce on f. 67v is dated 1284. The paleographical and codicological features are consistent with a late-13th-century dating; this dating is also supported by the fact that the colophon on f. 156r uses a formula for the living for Perez ben Elijah, of Corbeil, who died around 1297. The 1384 date of the second letter of divorce on fol. 68r was presumably a mistake
This place name (שנשפורין מתא דיתבא על נהר אוזון) is mentioned in the letters of divorce on ff. 67v-68r.

history.provenance: There are numerous ownership inscriptions throughout the manuscript including, on f. 48r, the name Asher/Anshel ben Shelomoh Zalman Heilprin אשר/אנשל בן שלמה זלמן היילפרין. On f. 160r, Anshel ben Shelomoh's brother-in-law notes that he received the book from Anshel on the occasion of his marriage to Anshel's sister, Elka (עלקא); he subsequently recorded the birth of his children, including Roza (רוזה) who was born in 1709 as the family was fleeing from the plague in the town of 'ליסא', probably Leszno (German: Lissa) in western Poland. There are further registers of births by earlier owners dated between 1530 and 1547 at the beginning of the manuscript.
history.acquisition: It belonged to David ben Abraham Oppenheim's (1664-1736) library, which was purchased by the Bodleian Library in 1829.

recordHist.source: Manuscript description based on Catalogue of the Hebrew Manuscripts in the Bodleian Library, vol. I, by Adolf Neubauer, Oxford 1886, No. 874, Catalogue of the Hebrew manuscripts in the Bodleian Library; Supplement of Addenda and Corrigenda to Vol. I, No. 874, and on the data of the Institute of Microfilmed Hebrew Manuscripts, National Library of Israel with additional enhancements by the cataloguer.

availability.p: Entry to read in the Library is permitted only on presentation of a valid reader's card (for admissions procedures contact Bodleian Admissions). Contact specialcollections.enquiries@bodleian.ox.ac.uk for further information on the availability of this manuscript.

listBibl.bibl: Neubauer, Adolf, Catalogue of the Hebrew Manuscripts in the Bodleian Library (Oxford, 1886), no. 874.

Beit-Arié, Malachi and R.A. May (eds), Catalogue of the Hebrew manuscripts in the Bodleian Library; Supplement of Addenda and Corrigenda to Vol. I (A. Neubauer’s Catalogue) (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1994), no. 874.
Galinsky, Judah D., The Making of a Medieval Bestseller: Rabbi Isaac of Corbeil's Pillars of Exile and its Readers (Oxford University Press, 2025), pp. 114, 139-141.
